content.theorem: The covering radius of the Eisenstein integer lattice is √3/3 ≈ 0.5774, which is optimal among all 2D lattices
content.proof: The Voronoï cell of the hexagonal lattice is a regular hexagon with circumradius √3/3. The covering radius equals this circumradius — the maximum distance from any point in the Voronoï cell to its center. For the square lattice (Gaussian integers), the covering radius is √2/2 ≈ 0.7071. Ratio: √3/3 ÷ √2/2 = √(2/3) ≈ 0.8165, an improvement of ~18.4% in radius, ~22% in area.
content.benchmark: Measured max snap error: 0.5764 (benchmark). Theoretical maximum: 0.5774. The gap (0.0010) is floating-point precision. 10M random points tested.
content.caveat: The 22% improvement is in covering area, not radius. The radius improvement is ~18.4%. Both are significant. The covering radius proof requires Voronoï cell analysis.
